title: StressAudit-SM: A Compact Robustness-Audit Protocol for Schema Matching Under Metadata Stress
description: This project proposes a compact evaluative protocol for auditing schema matchers under deterministic metadata stress rather than a new schema-matching method. It fixes one CPU-feasible split and one execution matrix, then measures accuracy degradation, ranking stability, calibration transfer, and abstention utility on stressed public schema pairs.
motivation: The earlier version claimed too much novelty and mixed incompatible protocols. Prior work already covers noisy-schema robustness (He and Chang, 2005), uncertainty-aware schema matching (Prompt-Matcher, 2024), and context-stress evaluation (ConStruM, 2026). The remaining gap is a small, reproducible, CPU-only audit recipe that evaluates these failure modes together on the same fixed public schema pairs.
proposed_approach: Build StressAudit-SM as a deterministic audit layer over 52 public schema pairs with one fixed split used everywhere: 12 clean development pairs, 8 stressed development pairs, and 32 held-out test pairs. Run exactly three core methods in the main paper, a lexical matcher, Similarity Flooding, and the PTLM baseline from Schema Matching using Pre-Trained Language Models, under four conditions: clean, header-stress, context-stress, and composite-stress. Because the local Valentine archive exposes no usable descriptions, context-stress is implemented as shared removal of table-name, sibling-schema, and type-context metadata across all methods rather than description masking. Fit one logistic calibrator and one abstention threshold per method on clean development data, use stressed development pairs only for one transfer check, report paired-bootstrap uncertainty over the 32 test pairs, and treat any no-ranking-change outcome as an explicit negative result rather than hidden protocol drift.

related_work.title: Making holistic schema matching robust: an ensemble approach
related_work.difference: This is the key older robustness precedent, so the proposal no longer claims to be the first study of robustness under noisy metadata. Its distinction is a compact modern evaluation recipe that adds ranking stability, calibration transfer, and abstention analysis on fixed public pairwise tasks.

related_work.title: Cost-Aware Uncertainty Reduction in Schema Matching with GPT-4: The Prompt-Matcher Framework
related_work.authors: Longyu Feng, Huahang Li, Chen Jason Zhang
related_work.difference: Prompt-Matcher already makes uncertainty a first-class concern in schema matching. StressAudit-SM differs by being method-agnostic and explicitly testing whether confidence estimates learned on clean metadata remain reliable under deterministic stress.

related_work.title: ConStruM: A Structure-Guided LLM Framework for Context-Aware Schema Matching
related_work.authors: Houming Chen, Zhe Zhang, H. V. Jagadish
related_work.difference: ConStruM is the closest recent overlap on context-stress evaluation, but it is an LLM-oriented context-packing framework with a narrow benchmark. StressAudit-SM instead offers a broader pairwise audit protocol spanning classical and PTLM-style matchers under a shared CPU-feasible matrix.

related_work.title: Valentine: Evaluating Matching Techniques for Dataset Discovery
related_work.authors: Christos Koutras, George Siachamis, Andra Ionescu, Kyriakos Psarakis, Jerry Brons, Marios Fragkoulis, Christoph Lofi, Angela Bonifati, Asterios Katsifodimos
related_work.difference: Valentine is the relevant evaluation-harness predecessor. StressAudit-SM builds on that evaluation style but adds deterministic stress transforms, calibration-transfer checks, and abstention analysis rather than proposing a new clean benchmark.

related_work.title: Schema Matching using Pre-Trained Language Models
related_work.authors: Yunjia Zhang, Avrilia Floratou, Joyce Cahoon, Subru Krishnan, Andreas C. Muller, Dalitso Banda, Fotis Psallidas, Jignesh M. Patel
related_work.difference: PTLM provides the modern semantic baseline in the main protocol. The proposal uses it to test whether clean-metadata gains survive header and context stress under a strict CPU budget.

hypothesis: On a fixed set of public schema pairs, deterministic header stress and shared structural-context stress may change method rankings, degrade calibration models fit on clean metadata, and increase the utility of abstention at fixed coverage, but the study is explicitly allowed to conclude a negative result if those changes do not materialize.
success_criteria: At least one held-out method ordering changes between clean and a stressed condition.
At least one method shows materially worse expected calibration error or Brier score under stress after clean-only calibration.
Selective abstention improves precision at the preregistered coverage target under at least one stress condition.
The main protocol of 52 pairs, 3 methods, and 4 conditions completes within the stated 2-core and 8-hour CPU budget.
If the main matrix cannot complete within 8 hours, the proposal's feasibility claim is considered refuted rather than repaired by changing the protocol.
